quiz_metadata.title: One-Way ANOVA Pre-Test
quiz_metadata.description: Answer the following questions based on your current understanding. You are not expected to have prior knowledge of One-Way ANOVA. Answer to the best of your ability.

shared_stimulus.text: A researcher is studying whether three different study environments — a quiet library, a coffee shop, and a shared dorm room — affect student performance on a 50-point statistics quiz. Forty-five students are randomly assigned to one of the three environments, with 15 students in each group. After the quiz, the group averages are: Library = 38.2 (SD = 4.1), Coffee Shop = 34.7 (SD = 3.9), Dorm Room = 31.5 (SD = 4.4).

questions.title: Identifying Within-Group Variability
questions.question_text: Which of the following best represents within-group variability in this study?

answers.text: The difference between the Library mean (38.2) and the Dorm Room mean (31.5)
The spread of individual quiz scores among students studying in the Coffee Shop
The overall average score across all 45 students
The difference between the highest and lowest group means

questions.title: Identifying Between-Group Variability
questions.question_text: Which of the following best represents between-group variability in this study?

answers.text: The standard deviation within the Library group (SD = 4.1)
The range of scores among all 45 individual students
The differences among the three group means (38.2, 34.7, and 31.5)
The average standard deviation across the three groups

questions.title: F-Statistic Structure
questions.question_text: In a One-Way ANOVA, the F-statistic is calculated as:

answers.text: Within-group variability ÷ Between-group variability
Between-group variability ÷ Within-group variability
Sample mean ÷ Standard deviation
Total variability ÷ Number of groups

questions.title: Reasoning About a Small F
questions.question_text: Suppose the three study environment groups all had very similar quiz averages (35.1, 35.4, 35.2), but students within each group still had widely different scores. What would you expect the F-statistic to look like?

answers.text: Large, because there is a lot of total variability
Large, because within-group variability is high
Small, because between-group variability is low relative to within-group variability
Equal to 1, because the means are approximately the same

questions.title: Interpreting a Small F
questions.question_text: A researcher runs a One-Way ANOVA and gets a small F-statistic. Which is the most likely explanation?

answers.text: The group means are very different from each other
The observations within each group are tightly clustered around their group mean
The between-group differences are large compared to the within-group spread
The within-group variability is large relative to the differences between group means

questions.title: Role of Within-Group Variability
questions.question_text: Why does within-group variability matter when evaluating whether differences between group means are meaningful?

answers.text: It determines how many groups can be compared in a single ANOVA
Large within-group variability makes it harder to detect real differences between group means, because individual variation obscures the signal
Within-group variability inflates the between-group differences, making the F-statistic appear larger
It has no effect on the F-statistic — only the group means matter
